speaker: interviewer
text: --your flat in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we could move in there, which was extremely lucky, because most people had lost everything. And-- and there were not that many people who had come back, unfortunately. But they had to really find a new-- new place to live, and it was very different. It was a lot of war damage, as well, in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Otto Frank, for instance, he went to live with Miep, who was an employee who used to help hide his family. And that was around the corner from us. So one day, the bell rang, and Otto stood there, and the Red Cross had sent information out of people who had come back, because it was all registered, and so that people could get in touch with each other to find out if they knew about their family or friends. So he came to us and asked if he had perhaps seen his family. Then be told him that we had met him, and we had talked about it, and he hadn't-- he had forgotten about it. He couldn't remember at all that he had seen my mother or me. And he became a very good friend, then. He came often to us and talked to us, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still hoping that our families would come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, about six weeks after we had come back to Holland, we got notices from the Red Cross. We got the notice that Heinz and [? Fetty ?] had died in Mauthausen, beginning of May, just a few days before the liberation. And Otto Frank got notices that his wife had died in Auschwitz and Anne and Margot in Bergen-Belsen-- not to expect day, sometime in March or April, so it was well quite late in the war. And then really a new hardship begun for us and for Otto and for all the people who only survived part of the family. We had some money, but very little. So my mother said, well, we'll-- I have to try to find work, and we'll have to see how we can get through it. And this Mr. Rosenberg, he had a tie factory. And he said he could use my mother as a sort of secretary, bookkeeper. Well, my mother had never, ever worked in her life, had no idea about typing or bookkeeping. But she would tried to do that. And she used to work. She came home always completely upset because she made many, many mistakes, and she hid all the paper with the bad typing, which she brought back in a bag, which she didn't dare to show. And so she was quite a nervous wreck each time. And she still had to clean the flat when she came home in the evening and to do her shopping. So she had a very, very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I was 16. And I had missed out a lot of my schooling, partly before the war in-- in Holland. There'd been talks-- all the different languages, it was very difficult. And then in hiding I'd done some school work, but not much. And so Otto said to my mother-- I didn't want to go to school-- And Otto said, well, the one thing you can give Eva is a good education. So she definitely has to go back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So reluctantly, we went to see the school where my brother had been-- the Amsterdam Lyceum, which was a very, very good school. And we spoke with the headmaster there. It was a Professor Koenig, who was very, very anti-Nazi and anti-German and very pro-Jews, a very wonderful man. And he said-- I wasn't at all to go to this kind of school-- he said, of course, Eva can come and we'll give her extra lessons and we'll see that she can make out. So that was the other end of town. We were Amsterdam side-- the new side. And this was Amsterdam side in the [DUTCH], which was an old part of the town. So I had to go over on the bicycle to school. And it was very difficult for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I found it difficult to associate with the other children who had been at homes, or-- because I had it, of course, difficult. So I wanted to tell what had happened to me, but nobody was interested. And this is a big problem which a lot of survivors had, that nobody wanted to listen to anything what we had to tell. And we were full of it, and we wanted to speak about it very desperately. And we just couldn't. And the only friend I made was a-- was a girl who was called Eva, as well. And she a Jewish girl, but she had been in Indonesia in Dutch-- in Java, who had been captured by the Japanese and had been in a Japanese prison of war camp. So we had something in common. So we didn't speak much about our experience, but we felt we had gone through the same thing. And so her-- she had a sister and a father. And they all survived. But they had a terrible, bad luck. Her father was-- when he still had business things to attend to in Indonesia, he was killed on-- in a plane crash. And so, you know, that was very bad for her, after having survived the wars and being killed that way. And we became good friends. And I've still been in touch with her very recently.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, it was Kinsberger. But then she married-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew the-- a Jewish boy. But I've forgotten his name now. But I've been in-- I knew him as well, quite well. He looked a bit like Danny Kaye, and we always used to call him Danny. So his name was something quite different. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There started to be a little Jewish community, partly people who had come back from different concentration camps and partly, as well, Jews who had been in hiding. There was another Jewish family with three sons-- Solomons. And their middle son, Hank Solomon, he always came to us. He liked my mother and it was-- and reminded my mother of Heinz. And we got on very well. And a lot of young people came to the house from school, but you know, it as not-- we were not a family. And I was really-- there was a very-- instead of being a happy times that I survived, it was a very unhappy time. And that my father and brother hadn't survived is something I couldn't come to terms with at all. So we had hit notice from the Red Cross. I just didn't believe it. And I was convinced that one day my father would turn up. And one did hear stories that people who had escaped somewhere and had been in Russia and came back again. And I was convinced my father would turn up. And each time I heard a taxi pull up outside, I rushed to the window and thought, well, my father was here. And it, of course, never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked very hard at school, and I became a very good pupil, in comparison to that I was very bad before. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I quite enjoyed learning. Running But as I said, I wasn't happy. And the children felt that I was miserable, and they made me head girl, which I did, but again, not-- my heart wasn't in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when I had two more years to go-- I don't actually know why, because we were not that poor-- but my mother said, it's better that you learn already a profession, so that when you finish school, that you can earn money. And I didn't really know. I didn't want to learn anything. I didn't want to be anything. And my mother said, you're a bit arty. And I think you should be a photographer. And I didn't particularly want to be a photographer, but I didn't know anything else, and I was a very obedient child. And I said, oh, all right, I'll be a photographer. And so we knew somebody in town who had a studio, and they were doing art photography. And for-- they made slides for courses which was used in history at art. Let's say they were doing Michelangelo, so we went to the museum to take all the picture which the professor was going to use then later. And that's where I learned taking photos and printing and-- and everything,

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but I had to do is this together with school. So my mother went to Professor-- he was Professor Koenig, the headmaster-- and asked him if I would get free the afternoons to go to that studio. So he said, well, I must have a medical certificate that I could physically cope with that after all I'd been through at the camp, and you know, it might be a lot of strain. He actually advised against it, but I wanted to do it, and my mother said I had to do it, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a medical certificate, and I also had [INAUDIBLE]. I must say, after the war I had lot of trouble with my digestion. And that was for about two years. I was on a strict diet, just noodles and semolina and dry potatoes. I couldn't eat anything else. Partly perhaps it was because I had overeaten too much. And that went on for many years and a very nervous stomach. I had diarrhea as soon as I had an exam or anything like that. But after a few years, that passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I did that. And at this studio, there was a customer who had been to Spain. And he had taken hundreds of photographs, but he had never printed them. And he said to my boss, is there perhaps somebody who could work in the evening and print-- I've got all the equipment-- and print all those photographs for me? I'll pay, of course. And-- and so he asked me if I want to do that. And I said, yes, I'll do that. So after work, I went home first, to eat, and then I went on the bicycle in the night to this man, who was quite far again. And I worked a few hours. I printed perhaps three, four, at least, printing. And then I bicycled home at midnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that I remember very clearly. I fell in my bed. And I said, I'm so exhausted. I'm so exhausted. I'm sure I'll just die from exhaustion in the night. I was so tired. But I wouldn't say, I won't do it. You see, this is-- this was my survivor's instinct still. I-- I wasn't going to give up. I had to do that. And it-- I am sure-- I can't remember exactly, but it can't have been the money, because you know, by that time, my grandparents had got in touch with us. And they did send us, and we got as well from the American joined that sent clothes. So it was not because we didn't have the money to buy clothes, because there was nothing to buy. So you know, we got help from all directions. And my mother earned, after all. So I don't know why I did that. But I did this for quite a long time. And then we had the matriculation exam. And then the headmaster, Dr. Koenig said, I must give up now my work in the studio for the time the exam goes. And I did give this up. And I did very well. I got very-- I passed everything, and I got through very well. And in the meantime, Otto had become quite busy with the Jewish reform movement. And he went to England to conferences. There was an international reform conference here in London. And he said to me, it would be nice if you would come with me. You could become-- so I wasn't involved in synagogue or anything-- he said, you could become the youth representative. And I was very, very shy. I'm sure I was no use to be a representative. But he wanted to take me out, because he knew I wasn't happy. And he thought the traveling might do me good. And there was a conference at the end of the year-- from my school year. And we were going to get the certificates at school. And there was a big celebration. And we were going up and take the certificate in ourselves. And I didn't dare to tell the headmasters that I won't be there. So I said, I'm just not going. And I was going to England. And then I had won a prize-- a French prize-- because I was good in French, because I had learned French in Belgium. And the French consul was handing over a book to the best person [INAUDIBLE]. And when I had to go out and receive that, and he called out and I wasn't there. And he was very, very-- when I came back then, and he had phoned my mother then, and he said, I must come. And he was very, very cross with me, that he said, you could have told me. But just to go off without telling. And-- and that very embarrassing. I felt very bad about it, because he was very, very good to me. But you know, it was one of the things you do, and you don't think about consequences. And I got very, very close with Otto. He-- he was a wonderful man. And he had published, in the meantime, already, the diary. And it was published in Holland. It wasn't a great success. Again, people weren't really interested much. But was-- they had difficulty getting 500 copies out. But-- and I noticed that he was very, very on good terms with my mother. They were very, very nice together. And then, when I finished school I-- I went to work full-time in this-- in that studio. But again, I wasn't really very happy. And I had as a boyfriend, this Hank from the three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: He always fetched me from my work and took me out, and so-- but I wasn't particularly interested in boys at all, still. And then Otto said, perhaps you want to go to England for a year. So I said, yes. Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he knew somebody who had that photographic studio here, called [? Roban ?] Studio in Bloomsbury. And he said, I'll speak to her and see if you can go there. And he arranged it. And I went here. And I knew another boy who had an uncle in Holland, Harry Jacobi. He became later a rabbi. And he lived in England. He went as a kindertransport child to England. But his-- his uncle lived in Holland. And we knew him. And this boy found me a boarding-- a boarding house in London in Cricklewood in [INAUDIBLE] Road. And so I moved in there and went to work every morning. And I was very happy. And England was at the time-- it was very bad still. There was big rationing. You had one egg a week and two lamb chops or little bit of meat and very little food. But there was a spirit of rebuilding. The English--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '51. The English are very good at sticking together when things are difficult. For instance, well, you know that here, but in-- you could eat, for instance, in a Lyon Corner House. But you were only allowed to eat for two and six. Even if you were very rich, you could perhaps get food for 10 pounds, but you were not allowed. You were only allowed for two and six to eat in a restaurant, so that the rich couldn't eat out more than the poor. So-- and you felt everywhere, everything was bombed. The city was in a terrible state. But there was a wonderful spirit. They had won the war, and they were going to be on top of it. In Holland, you didn't have that. You had more a depression. And so I-- I felt good here. And in this boarding house lived another-- they were all young people. One was a butcher-- a Jewish butcher. And another one was a young men from Israel, who had come to study economics. And he loved chocolates. And my mother had sent me from Holland-- in Holland it was then, in '51, better than it was in England. There was-- you had sweet coupons, but was very little. And my mother kept on sending me chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this Israeli boy, Zvi, was fond of chocolate. So every evening, he came knocking on my door-- anymore chocolate from Holland? And that's how we became friends. And eventually, I was interested in him. And we went out together. And we went Sundays on hikes, and he was hear really only for a year, and he was going to go back to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, Israel appealed to me. I thought it's completely new break. And kibbutz, I've heard-- I've heard from kibbutz. And I was rather communistic inclined. And this sort of fitted in with my idea of life. So I was quite interested, and he said he was going to go back. And then we started to make a life together. And then my mother and Otto had come over already and met Zvi, and he liked him very much. They could talk together. He was very intelligent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then my mother came over. And I said to Zvi, you must ask my mother if you can marry me. So we went to walk on Hampstead Heath. And he was very shy, and he said, I want to-- after she talked with him for a while, he said, I want to ask you, may I marry your daughter? And my mother said-- she knew because had told her he's going to ask her. So she said, yes, but of course, it's up to me to decide. It's-- I have to say yes or no. So Zvi said, well, will you marry me? And I said I have to think about it. Suddenly it it was daunting on me. It was a bit of a shock.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my mother said, well, if she decides, you must promise me one thing-- that you won't take her to Israel, because I don't fly. And she's after all, the only thing I've got left. And England is far enough. And you know, Israel, I will never see her, or hardly ever. And that would be terrible. And I saw too, what-- you know, it would be terrible to go so far. And my husband promised it then. And then we decided to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Otto came over, I said to him-- I remember we went to a Lyons' Cornerhouse in Marble Arch. And we had a meal there. And I said to him, you know, it's all right if I marry Zvi here, but what will happen to my mother? She'll be left all alone there. And it's a bit-- I'm a bit upset and worried about it. So he said, well, don't worry about it. I'm going to marry her. So she didn't know, but I knew already that he was going to marry her, but she didn't know yet. So he went back then to Amsterdam, and he proposed to her, and she accepted, because she was already in love. She told me later she-- she liked him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so a year after we got married, they got married. But we went over to marry in Amsterdam, because we didn't really know many people here. And we had many more friends there. And this Hank who I was a little bit-- was a bit my boyfriend-- he was very upset. But he came to the wedding. And he still came later always to my mother for evening meal. And then my mother introduced him to [? Eunid, ?] you know, from [INAUDIBLE], a girl who had come. And they did get married. And they're still married, and they have two children. And they're quite happy. So-- and I am in touch with them. And my mother is very much in touch with them. So he-- you know, they think this is her doing that they found each other, because they didn't really know each other. So-- and then, well, then we stayed here. And my husband felt, after a while, quite resentful that he couldn't really go to Israel. And at that time, people who had left Israel were treated as sort of betrayal. And that was a bit difficult for him, then. I mean, he would never have gone back on his word. And then, by then I was very happy here, And I don't think I would have wanted to go. I'd gone through so many countries and, you know, languages and countries. And I felt quite at home here. And I said to him then I would want to go to another country, start again with a different language. So-- so that was-- that was that then. And Zvi's mother still lived in Israel, but she went then to Germany, partly as well to be nearer us, but as well because she got German repatriation money. And-- and another bad thing first happened. I went to-- I did work a bit as a photographer. But it was difficult to get jobs then. And so I did freelance photography. And I looked at all the adverts and did find a job. And then, in my spare time, I went to different museums for lectures. There were free lectures. I was very interested in-- that was geology. And once, at a break, I met a man who said he does palm reading. And could he read my palm. And he read my palm, and he said, you-- you will have great difficulty getting children. You might not even get children. And that-- I was terribly upset about that, because like I told you, I thought to live is continuing with a family. And I was-- and he told me all kind of things, which were true. But this shocked me terribly, and a said to my husband-- were were only young married, and we-- he earned 10 pounds. He had finished his-- his lecture. And he was a stockbroker, and he earned very little. And we really were very poor. We lived in terrible accommodation in Cricket Road, rented. And we couldn't really afford children at the time. But I said to my husband, we must immediately try to have children, because I'm very, very worried. And we tried, and nothing happened. And then I went to my GP and said, you know, we want a family and doesn't work. So he sent me to a hospital, and I had done tests. And they found there was something not working properly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was very, very upset and worried. And then they say, well, we don't think it's serious. We'll put you on a six months sort of a hormone course, which hopefully will put it right. And I went on that, and after the six months, I conceived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and after nine months, Caroline was born. And I was, of course, extremely happy and greatly relieved. And after-- 18 months afterwards, Jackie was born. And but I wanted still-- I wanted a big, big family. But Zvi was-- so he was a good father, and he was interested. He was very, very occupied making his career. And I didn't really have any help from him with the family. And then he took a job on-- as quite high up in Switzerland with Bank Leumi for an Israeli bank. And when Jackie was two months, he went to Switzerland to find accommodation. And I was going to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was on my own with the two little children. And it was quite difficult. And then, after six months-- so he came over occasionally, and I went over once-- after six months, we moved over to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was supposed to be for two years, but we stayed six years. And it was already this house, but we let it at the time. And so we lived for six-- six years in Switzerland, which was quite nice, because my mother and Otto had moved to Switzerland as well, to Basel, because Otto's family, his mother and brother and sister had lived in Switzerland. And Otto and my mother were not very happy to stay in Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we're going to stop there.
